behaviors.description: The speech-to-text output preserves speaker labels so that the summary can attribute statements to specific attendees.
behaviors.justification: The failure boundary is real speech-to-text API behavior. A mock could return perfectly formatted speaker labels, but the real API might return output in an unexpected format, lack labels on certain audio characteristics, or fail entirely on realistic meeting audio with overlapping speech and background noise. Testing requires real HTTP calls to the STT API and realistic (or real) meeting audio to expose format/capability mismatches that wouldn't surface with a mock.
behaviors.test_description: Verify that a real speech-to-text API call on realistic or real meeting audio returns a transcript with speaker labels that can be reliably extracted. The test should confirm that speaker identity is preserved in the output and that attribution to attendees is possible. Failure detected when the service attempts to attribute statements but speaker information is missing, garbled, or in an unexpected format.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Mocking the STT API (Unit/Integration) would hide real API behavior—the real service might return no labels on complex audio, or labels in a different format. Realistic audio characteristics matter: test audio with clear, separated speakers might mislead a test, while real meeting recordings with crosstalk and noise expose the actual API capability.

behaviors.description: The structured summary contains at least one action item for every attendee who was assigned a task during the meeting.
behaviors.justification: The failure is fundamentally LLM comprehension—a real LLM might miss subtle or implicit task assignments that a mock would never miss. The LLM's understanding of meeting language (e.g., 'Can you own the vendor selection?' or 'We need someone to update the docs') is non-deterministic and variable across runs. Only real LLM calls will expose whether the model reliably extracts all action items from realistic meeting transcripts.
behaviors.test_description: Verify that a real LLM, when given a meeting transcript containing attendee names and task assignments (both explicit and implied), reliably extracts all assigned action items and attributes them to the correct attendees. Failure is detected when an attendee mentioned in a task assignment ('Sarah, can you follow up with legal?') is omitted from the action items section, or when assignments are created but attributed to the wrong person.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: A mock LLM would always return perfect output with all action items captured; it cannot reveal the real model's failure modes. Only a real LLM call exposes whether the model understands implicit task language, handles ambiguity correctly, and maintains consistent attribution across a realistic meeting transcript.

behaviors.description: Key decisions mentioned in the transcript appear in the 'decisions' section of the summary, not buried in the narrative.
behaviors.justification: The failure is how the real LLM categorizes information. The model might misinterpret what constitutes a 'key decision' versus background discussion, or place decisions in the wrong field. This is a semantic understanding problem that only manifests with a real LLM—a mock could always return the correct structure, but the real model might confuse decisions with narrative or fail to parse the structured output format correctly.
behaviors.test_description: Verify that a real LLM correctly extracts key decisions from a meeting transcript and places them in the structured 'decisions' field rather than mixing them into the narrative or summary text. Failure is detected when a decision mentioned in the transcript (e.g., 'We decided to hire 3 engineers' or 'Budget approved for Q2 launch') appears only in narrative text or is missing from the decisions section altogether.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Mocking the LLM response would trivialize this test—the mock would always return perfect categorization. The real failure mode is semantic: does the LLM understand the distinction between 'we discussed this' and 'we decided on this'? Only real LLM behavior reveals misclassification.

behaviors.description: When the audio file is longer than 60 minutes, the service splits it into segments and processes them sequentially, merging the results into a single coherent summary.
behaviors.justification: This behavior requires multiple sequential processing steps: transcribe segment 1, summarize segment 1, transcribe segment 2, summarize segment 2, then merge/reconcile results (likely via another LLM call for coherence). Real failures emerge at the boundaries: segment order dependencies, context loss between segments, conflicting information in merged output, or timeout/resource exhaustion on real long audio. Testing a single segment (Agentic) misses merge failures; mocking any component (Unit/Integration/System) hides real behavior where segment summaries must fit together coherently.
behaviors.test_description: Verify that a meeting recording longer than 60 minutes is correctly segmented, each segment is independently transcribed and summarized, and the results are merged into a single coherent summary without losing information or introducing contradictions. Failure is detected if the merged summary contains inconsistent decisions, missing context from earlier segments, information loss at boundaries, or if segment processing order produces different results.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Sequential dependencies matter: segment 2 summarization might depend on context from segment 1, and merge coherence depends on LLM behavior across multiple runs. Testing only one segment or mocking the merge step would pass even if real sequential processing fails. A real long audio file exposes resource and timing issues that test audio doesn't.

behaviors.description: The full pipeline — upload audio, transcribe, summarize, deliver structured output — completes without manual intervention for a real meeting recording.
behaviors.justification: This is an end-to-end orchestration test: audio file handling (System), real STT API calls (System), real LLM summarization (Agentic), and integration between all steps (Workflow). Real failures occur at integration points—a file parse error on real audio, STT API output format unexpected by the parser, LLM timeout on long transcripts, or the final output format doesn't deserialize. Testing just the LLM in isolation (Agentic) misses orchestration failures; mocking any component hides the interaction failures that occur in production.
behaviors.test_description: Verify that a complete end-to-end pipeline execution with real meeting audio completes successfully without requiring manual intervention, remediation, or fallback steps. The pipeline must handle: file upload and validation, transcription API call and output parsing, LLM summarization and structured output generation, and result delivery. Failure is detected if any step fails (file format errors, API timeouts, output parsing exceptions, malformed structured output) or if the final result is unusable or incomplete.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: The phrase 'without manual intervention' implies all steps must work together—not just that one component succeeds. Testing only the LLM (Agentic) would miss failures in audio parsing, STT format handling, output serialization, or orchestration logic. Real meeting audio exposes edge cases (long duration, multiple speakers, background noise) that don't surface with test audio or mocks. Workflow is the minimum to catch integration failures.
